did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning algorithms, specifically LightGBM, Support Vector Machines (SVM), and HAAR Cascade classifiers, combined with GLCM for texture feature extraction, to perform preliminary identification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the application of machine learning algorithms (LightGBM, SVM, HAAR Cascade) to the HAM10000 dataset for skin lesion identification. It details a specific methodology including image preprocessing, feature extraction using GLCM, model training, and validation, and reports new results such as accuracy rates (90% for LightGBM, 100% for SVM and Cascade) and CPU usage metrics.

title: Preliminary Identification of Skin Lesions using Efficient Computational Learning Techniques
brief_summary: This study presents a preliminary identification of skin lesions using efficient computational learning techniques as an initial diagnostic stage. The researchers utilized the HAM10000 dataset, processing images through grayscale transformation and extracting texture features via the Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM). Three machine learning classifiers were evaluated: LightGBM, Support Vector Machine (SVM), and HAAR Cascade. The models were trained on 456 images and validated on 10 additional test images to assess accuracy and computational resource usage. The results showed that SVM and HAAR Cascade achieved 100% accuracy, while LightGBM achieved 90% accuracy. However, LightGBM was identified as the most efficient method, requiring only one minute of training time and significantly lower CPU usage compared to the other classifiers.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on the preliminary identification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The classification task distinguishes between 'skin lesions' (positive class) and 'skin sections without any affectation' or 'skin sections without abnormality' (negative class). Specific histological types from HAM10000 (e.g., melanoma, nevus) are not explicitly listed as separate classes in the experimental description, which treats the target as a binary 'lesion vs. no lesion' problem.
Used as the negative class in the binary classification task to distinguish from skin lesions. The dataset included 285 images of normal skin for training and 5 for validation.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions (positive class) versus normal skin sections without affectation (negative class).
all_task_types.details: The paper performs preliminary identification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The task is binary, distinguishing between 'skin lesions' and 'skin sections without any affectation' (or 'without abnormality'). Models evaluated include LightGBM, Support Vector Machine (SVM), and HAAR Cascade classifiers. Performance was measured by accuracy (hit rate) on a test set of 10 images (5 lesions, 5 normal skin). LightGBM achieved 90% accuracy, while SVM and HAAR Cascade achieved 100% accuracy.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, used as the source for the images in this study.
classes_used: Skin sections without any affectation / without abnormality (negative class)

use.training: 456 images (171 lesions, 285 normal) were used to train the LightGBM, SVM, and HAAR Cascade classifiers.
use.validation: 10 additional images (5 lesions, 5 normal) were used to validate the models and determine accuracy/hit rates.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy (hit rate), training time, and CPU usage metrics.

HAM10000.split: Training (456 images: 171 lesions, 285 normal) and Validation (10 images: 5 lesions, 5 normal)

LightGBM.details: Out of 5 skin lesion images, all 5 were effectively estimated. Out of 5 skin section images, 4 were effectively classified.

Support Vector Machine (SVM).details: Effective classification between classes was presented for both lesion and skin section images.

HAAR Cascade.details: Accurate prediction was achieved for both the 5 images with lesions and the 5 images of skin sections.

HAM10000.notes: LightGBM required the shortest learning time and significantly lower CPU usage (approximately 3 times lower) compared to SVM and cascaded classifiers, despite having lower accuracy (90% vs 100%).

architecture_used.main_model: LightGBM, Support Vector Machine (SVM), and HAAR Cascade classifiers
architecture_used.input_size: 75x75 pixels (for HAAR Cascade); Grayscale images (dimensions not specified for LightGBM/SVM feature vectors)

training_details.framework: Python with Pandas, OpenCV, Numpy, and Sklearn libraries
training_details.feature_extraction: Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) for texture features

classification_or_output_head.HAAR Cascade: Binary classification via successive filtering of scales and positions

preprocessing_techniques: Grayscale transformation using weighted sum of RGB components (I = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B)
Reduction of gray values and pixel combinations for GLCM calculation
Definition of angular orientations and distances between pixels for GLCM
Sample dimension resizing to 75x75 pixels for HAAR Cascade training
conclusions: The study successfully demonstrated the preliminary identification of skin lesions using efficient computational learning techniques (LightGBM, SVM, and HAAR Cascade) combined with GLCM texture feature extraction as an initial diagnostic stage.
LightGBM was identified as the most efficient classifier, requiring the shortest training time (1 minute) and significantly lower CPU usage (16.50%) compared to SVM (41%) and HAAR Cascade (54.25%).
Despite its efficiency, LightGBM achieved a 90% accuracy rate, whereas both SVM and HAAR Cascade achieved 100% accuracy on the validation set of 10 images.
The SVM and HAAR Cascade classifiers required substantially longer training times (approximately 40 minutes) and higher computational resources compared to LightGBM.

The proposed methodology using the HAM10000 dataset, grayscale transformation, and GLCM features is viable for distinguishing between skin lesions and normal skin sections, with LightGBM offering a favorable trade-off between performance and computational cost.
important_remarks: The validation set is extremely small (only 10 images: 5 lesions, 5 normal), which makes the reported 100% accuracy for SVM and HAAR Cascade and 90% for LightGBM statistically unreliable and prone to overfitting or chance.

The study treats the problem as a binary classification (lesion vs. normal skin) rather than distinguishing specific histological types (e.g., melanoma vs. nevus), limiting its clinical utility for differential diagnosis.

The dataset split is non-standard for HAM10000; the paper uses a custom subset of 456 training images and 10 validation images, preventing comparison with standard HAM10000 benchmarks and raising concerns about data leakage or selection bias.
No external validation was performed; the models were only tested on the small internal validation set from the same source dataset.
The paper does not report standard statistical significance tests, confidence intervals, or sensitivity/specificity metrics, relying solely on accuracy (hit rate) on a tiny sample.

The methodology focuses on computational efficiency (training time, CPU usage) rather than diagnostic robustness, which may not reflect real-world clinical performance where image quality and lesion variability are higher.

The use of grayscale transformation and GLCM features discards color information, which is critical for dermatological diagnosis, potentially limiting the model's ability to detect subtle lesion characteristics.
